description: The 12 Disney principles of animation, translated to low-resolution pixel art constraints. Original principles from Thomas & Johnston 1981; pixel-art adaptations from Pedro Medeiros, Saint11, AdamCYounis.

squash_and_stretch.concept: Object deforms to suggest weight and rigidity. Heavy = less deform; light = more.
squash_and_stretch.pixel_art_adaptation: At 16-32px, deformation is per-pixel chunky: a ball squashing flattens 2-3px on impact, stretches 1px in flight. Conserve area (squash 1px wide = stretch 1px tall).

anticipation.concept: Wind-up before main action. Eye sees the wind-up and predicts the action.
anticipation.pixel_art_adaptation: 1-2 frames of opposite-direction motion before action. E.g. attack: 1 frame leans BACK before swinging forward.

staging.concept: Pose/composition reads in silhouette. The frame's intent is clear at a glance.
staging.pixel_art_adaptation: Test by squinting - does each frame read as a distinct pose? If frames blur together, redraw the silhouette with more difference.
staging.tip: Each key frame should be readable from across the room.

straight_ahead_vs_pose_to_pose.concept: Two animation strategies. Straight-ahead = draw frame 1, then 2, then 3 sequentially. Pose-to-pose = draw key frames first, fill in-betweens.
straight_ahead_vs_pose_to_pose.pixel_art_adaptation: ALWAYS pose-to-pose for pixel art. Define keys (contact, passing, contact for walk) then fill. Straight-ahead drifts off-model.
straight_ahead_vs_pose_to_pose.key_pose_count: Walk: 4 keys. Attack: 3 keys (windup, contact, recover).

follow_through_overlapping_action.concept: Loose parts (cape, hair, weapon) continue after main body stops. Different parts move at different rates.
follow_through_overlapping_action.pixel_art_adaptation: Add 1-2 frames of cape/hair settling AFTER the body has stopped. Animate cape on its own layer with 1-2 frame offset from body.

slow_in_slow_out.concept: Most motion accelerates and decelerates - few things start/stop instantly.
slow_in_slow_out.pixel_art_adaptation: Frame durations vary: longer at extremes, shorter in middle. E.g. attack swing: 120ms windup, 60ms swing, 120ms hold.

arcs.concept: Real motion follows arcs, not straight lines. Hands swing in arcs, head bobs in arcs.
arcs.pixel_art_adaptation: When animating a hand attack: middle frame should be HIGHER than end-points (arc up over). Walk cycle head: bobs up at passing pose, down at contact.
arcs.test: Trace path of a feature (eye, hand, weapon tip) across all frames - should curve, not straight

secondary_action.concept: Secondary motions support main action without distracting.
secondary_action.pixel_art_adaptation: Easy at 32+px (cape, breath). Hard at 16px (often have to skip). Pick ONE secondary at low res.

timing.concept: Frame count and duration define mood. Fast = energetic. Slow = heavy/dramatic.
timing.pixel_art_adaptation: Standard frame durations: 100ms = quick (run), 150ms = normal (walk), 200ms+ = heavy/idle.

weight_table.boss_telegraph: 500-1000ms per frame (lets player see it coming)

exaggeration.concept: Push the action beyond reality for clarity at small sizes.
exaggeration.pixel_art_adaptation: At 16-32px, subtle is invisible. Exaggerate windups (lean back FURTHER), squash MORE on impact, hair flap WIDER.
exaggeration.rule_of_thumb: If a real-life motion is X, pixel-art version is 2X to 3X.

solid_drawing.concept: Each frame feels like a solid 3D form, not a flat shape.
solid_drawing.pixel_art_adaptation: Maintain consistent shading direction (light source) across all frames. Don't redistribute shadow per frame randomly. Lock the light position.
solid_drawing.common_mistake: Drawing each frame fresh with different shadow placement -> object feels rubber

appeal.concept: Charisma / readability of design. The character should be interesting to look at.
appeal.pixel_art_adaptation: Strong silhouette > internal detail. Squint test: can you tell character apart from others by silhouette alone?

frame_economy.rule: Each frame must EARN its place. 4 great frames > 12 mushy frames. Cut frames that don't change silhouette meaningfully.
frame_economy.test: If you delete a frame and the animation still reads, delete it.

shape_keys_over_motion_keys.rule: Pixel art is shape animation, not motion animation. Each frame is a distinct REDRAWN shape, not an interpolated position.
shape_keys_over_motion_keys.implication: Don't try to lerp between frames. Hand-craft each pose.

hold_frames_at_extremes.rule: Extend duration of extreme poses (highest jump, deepest crouch). Eye lingers on extremes.
hold_frames_at_extremes.example: Jump cycle: peak frame = 200ms, ascending frames = 80ms each.

color_swap_for_states.rule: Use palette swap for state changes (hurt = red flash, frozen = blue tint) instead of redrawing pose.
color_swap_for_states.implementation: Same frames, different palette. Cheaper than new frames.

1px_threshold.rule: If motion is sub-1-pixel, it doesn't exist. Either move 1px or move 0.
1px_threshold.implication: Slow motion can't slow infinitely - eventually you must just hold a frame.

common_pitfalls: Animating without anticipation -> action feels limp
Equal frame durations -> mechanical feel (use variable timing)
Light source shifting per frame -> sprite feels rubbery
Too many in-between frames at 16px -> visual mush
Idle that doesn't loop seamlessly -> visible 'pop' every cycle
Walk where head doesn't bob -> robotic gliding
